title: A24 Knows You're Mad About the Google AI Collab
gist: Google DeepMind announced a $75 million research partnership with indie film studio A24 via A24 Labs, triggering backlash from fans who see the deal as a betrayal of A24's anti-establishment brand identity. Critics argue the partnership represents Google using culturally credible artists to normalize and 'launder' the reputation of generative AI, while A24 frames it as a way to shape tools before they are imposed on creators.

themes.theme: Google-A24 DeepMind Deal Structure and Rationale

insights.claim: Google DeepMind signed a $75 million research partnership with A24 to create new filmmaking tools, channeled through A24's technology startup A24 Labs, overseen by cofounder Scott Belsky.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: A major AI lab is making a large-scale capital commitment to embed itself within prestige film production, signaling AI's push into creative industries is accelerating beyond pilots.
insights.quote: the tech giant is teaming up with A24 to create new filmmaking "tools," as part of A24's technology startup, A24 Labs, overseen by cofounder Scott Belsky

insights.claim: A24 frames its DeepMind partnership as a seat-at-the-table strategy, arguing that engaging with AI development gives artists a voice in shaping tools rather than having them imposed from outside.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: This framing will likely become a standard justification for Hollywood-AI partnerships, masking commercial incentives with creative empowerment rhetoric.
insights.quote: This partnership exists because we want to dictate what tools get built for artists, and so they have a voice in shaping them rather than having tools handed to them.

insights.claim: A24's communications director stated the Google partnership is focused on workflow pain points behind the scenes rather than generating AI-native film content or licensing A24 IP to DeepMind users.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Investors should note A24 is positioning this as a B2B tool development play rather than an IP licensing revenue stream, limiting near-term monetization upside but reducing brand risk.
insights.quote: This partnership is about learning and helping pain points in workflows behind the scenes more than anything else.

themes.theme: AI Reputation Laundering Through Cultural Brands

insights.claim: Film critic Esther Rosenfield characterized Google's A24 deal as reputation laundering, arguing that AI companies partner with tastemaker studios to make it appear serious artists are using their tools when most are not.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: AI companies face a credibility deficit in creative markets and are using brand-adjacent partnerships as a substitute for product quality, which may inflate partnership valuations beyond fundamental value.
insights.quote: They're saying, 'We want to launder our reputation through you.' We want to make it look like serious artists are going to be making things with these tools. Because serious artists, by and large, aren't.

insights.claim: Media studies scholar Andrew DeWaard argues AI firms like Google deliberately pursue cultural partnerships to make AI feel inevitable and normalized, with culture serving as a vehicle for social normalization.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Cultural brand acquisitions and partnerships by AI companies should be read as normalization strategy, not pure product development, which has implications for antitrust and regulatory analysis.
insights.quote: They want to make AI feel inevitable. They want to make AI feel like it's everywhere. They want it to feel normal. Culture is part of that.

insights.claim: A new AI startup called Taste Labs raised $18.5 million with a stated goal of eliminating 'slop' and building AI systems with taste-making sensibilities, reflecting the industry's acknowledged deficit in aesthetic judgment.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: VC capital is flowing into AI taste differentiation as a competitive moat, suggesting future AI product competition may shift from raw capability to aesthetic output quality.
insights.quote: a new AI startup, literally called Taste Labs, recently secured $18.5 million in funding for its goal to "eliminate slop" and invest in AI clients with their own taste-making sensibilities

themes.theme: Hollywood-Silicon Valley Partnership Dynamics and Failures

insights.claim: Disney's $1 billion stake in OpenAI, which included licensing Mickey Mouse, Goofy, and C-3PO to Sora, collapsed when Sora was shut down, illustrating the fragility of large IP-AI licensing deals.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Early Hollywood-AI licensing megadeals have failed at execution, raising risk flags for valuing A24's DeepMind deal or similar arrangements at face value.
insights.quote: Late last year, Disney announced it was taking a $1 billion stake in OpenAI, including licensing characters like Mickey Mouse, Goofy, and C-3PO to the company's video generation model, Sora. A few months later, Sora itself was kaput, as was the deal.

insights.claim: Studios have begun distancing themselves from Luca Guadagnino's OpenAI founder biopic 'Artificial', suggesting AI association creates a chilling effect on mainstream Hollywood distribution decisions.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: AI brand toxicity is measurable in Hollywood deal flow, which constrains OpenAI and other AI companies' ability to use film as a soft power and marketing vehicle.
insights.quote: growing concerns that AI's capture of the film business has a chilling effect, as in the recent case of studios distancing themselves from Luca Guadagnino's biopic of OpenAI founder Sam Altman, Artificial

insights.claim: Some studios have sued AI companies for copyright infringement, positioning legal risk as a material overhang on AI-Hollywood partnership structures.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Copyright litigation against AI firms represents a structural legal risk for AI content-generation businesses that investors should price into valuations.
insights.quote: Some studios have sued AI companies for copyright infringement.

themes.theme: A24's Brand Value and Fan Backlash Risk

insights.claim: A24 has built a monetizable fan brand — selling caps, tote bags, and limited-edition tie-dye T-shirts — that is unique among American entertainment companies, making its brand equity unusually vulnerable to fan alienation.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: A24's brand-based revenue streams (merchandise, cultural cachet driving ticket sales) face asymmetric downside risk from AI backlash in a way that traditional studios without fandoms do not.
insights.quote: It is also the rare American entertainment company to have its own loyal groupies, who flex their own cinephilic bona fides with A24 caps, tote bags, and collectable, limited-edition tie-dye T-shirts.

insights.claim: Following the DeepMind announcement, A24 fans publicly threatened to pirate A24 films to erode revenues and declared the studio dead on social media, indicating measurable brand damage from the AI partnership.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Fan boycotts and piracy threats are a quantifiable early signal of brand erosion that could suppress A24's box office multipliers on future releases if sentiment persists.
insights.quote: comments under the trailer were littered with criticism lobbed at A24, from fans posting tombstones and declaring the death of the company, to promises of illegally pirating the movie

insights.claim: Film critic Esther Rosenfield described A24 as having sold the feeling of being hip and cutting-edge the way Disney sells nostalgia, identifying cultural currency rather than IP as A24's core asset.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Cultural currency assets depreciate rapidly under reputational stress, making A24's valuation more sensitive to sentiment shifts than traditional studio valuations based on library IP.
insights.quote: In the same way Disney sells nostalgia, A24 has sold the feeling of being very hip, and cutting-edge, for as long as they've been around.

insights.claim: Andrew DeWaard argues the A24-DeepMind deal is not a break from A24's prior practices, noting A24's cofounder Daniel Katz previously led film financing at Guggenheim Partners and the company received a significant cash injection from Thrive Capital, which has also invested heavily in OpenAI.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: A24's financial backer Thrive Capital has deep OpenAI exposure, meaning the DeepMind deal may reflect investor-level coordination rather than an independent strategic choice by A24 management.
insights.quote: In 2024, the company received a significant cash injection from Thrive Capital, which has also invested heavily in OpenAI.

themes.theme: Backrooms as Cultural Backdrop and Irony of the Deal

insights.claim: Backrooms, an A24 horror film thematically read as a critique of generative AI, passed $300 million at the global box office, making it A24's biggest hit and providing the commercial context against which A24 announced its AI partnership.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: The juxtaposition reveals A24 monetized AI-skeptical cultural sentiment while simultaneously partnering with a major AI lab, which may sustain fan anger and intensify the reputational risk of the deal.
insights.quote: Recently passing $300 million at the global box office, Backrooms has become the biggest hit yet for its buzzy boutique producer and distributor, the New York company A24.

themes.theme: AI's Taste Deficit as Competitive and Market Problem

insights.claim: Generative AI outputs are widely described as 'slop' because AI systems lack the capacity for aesthetic judgment — they cannot discern good from bad or beautiful from ugly — which has prompted technologists to seek taste acquisition through cultural partnerships.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: AI's aesthetic quality gap is driving M&A and partnership strategies toward culturally credible brands rather than toward technical improvements, which is a structural market distortion investors should track.
insights.quote: generative AI clients and large language models are not human, they cannot judge, or discern good and bad, ugly or beautiful, cool or boring

insights.claim: Technologists are pursuing AI-curated art exhibitions in San Francisco galleries and partnerships with tastemaker companies as substitutes for solving AI's fundamental aesthetic judgment deficit.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Cultural brand laundering via partnerships and exhibitions is a workaround, not a solution, to AI's quality gap — meaning the underlying product differentiation problem will persist even after high-profile deals.
insights.quote: it's precisely this more subtle, sophisticated, definitionally human element that technologists seem desperate to replicate, whether it be by hosting AI-"curated" art exhibitions in San Francisco galleries, or by simply partnering with creative companies whose brand is synonymous with taste

themes.theme: Silicon Valley Financier Networks Behind Creative Industry AI Deals

insights.claim: A24 Labs head Scott Belsky was named among the leaked participants in Peter Thiel's invite-only Dialog club, linking the DeepMind deal's architect to Silicon Valley's elite financier network.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: Personal financier network ties between A24 Labs leadership and Silicon Valley power brokers suggest the DeepMind deal may reflect relationship-driven deal sourcing as much as strategic product vision.
insights.quote: A24 Labs head Scott Belsky, who's at the center of the DeepMind deal, was among the recently leaked names linked to Silicon Valley financier Peter Thiel's invite-only club, Dialog.

insights.claim: A24 cofounder Daniel Katz previously led film financing at Guggenheim Partners, a firm with major investments in environmentally ruinous resource extraction, suggesting A24's independent-minded brand has always been separate from its financial ownership values.
insights.speaker: John Semley
insights.so_what: A24's brand independence has always been marketing, not structural — which means the DeepMind backlash may fade as fans compartmentalize brand from financial reality, as they have before.
insights.quote: A24's cofounder, Daniel Katz, previously led film financing at Guggenheim Partners, a global firm heavily invested in environmentally ruinous resource extraction.
